The PKGBUILD only defines source and metadata in its global scope, with no `pkgver()` function or other top-level code that would execute during `makepkg --nobuild --noprepare`. There are no suspicious network requests, obfuscated commands, or data exfiltration. The `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` functions are not run during this step, so any code within them is out of scope. No security issues are present in the context of this narrow safety gate.
</details>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a legitimate part of the Stockfish chess engine's tablebase probing implementation. It contains standard C++ code for reading and decompressing Syzygy tablebase files (`.rtbw` and `.rtbz`), encoding chess positions, and performing WDL/DTZ probes. All file operations are limited to opening and memory-mapping tablebase files from user-configured paths. There are no network requests, no obfuscated code, no base64 decoding, no execution of external commands, and no data exfiltration. The code follows normal packaging practices for a chess engine and does not exhibit any signs of a supply-chain attack.
</details>

LLM auditresponse for net.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `net.sh` is a standard helper for Stockfish to automatically download and validate Neural Network (NNUE) evaluation files. It retrieves files from two official Stockfish sources (tests.stockfishchess.org and github.com/official-stockfish) over HTTPS. It validates the downloaded file by comparing the first 12 hex characters of its SHA-256 hash to the filename. If validation fails, it deletes the file and tries the next URL. There is no obfuscated code, no exfiltration of data, no execution of downloaded code (only `mv` of the downloaded file, then validation), and no references to external hosts outside the project’s own infrastructure. The use of `rm` and `exit` is routine and appropriate for this type of build-time dependency fetching. No genuinely malicious behavior is present.
</details>
<summary>Standard Stockfish NNUE download script, safe.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a standard C++ source file for the Stockfish chess engine's benchmark functionality. It contains benchmark position data (FEN strings) and logic to generate UCI commands for running performance tests. There is no evidence of malicious behavior:

- No network requests (no `curl`, `wget`, or socket operations).
- No obfuscated or encoded commands (no `eval`, `base64`, or hex/octal encoded strings).
- No data exfiltration or backdoor mechanisms.
- No unexpected file operations beyond reading a user-specified FEN file (via `std::ifstream`) for benchmark positions, which is normal and expected functionality for a benchmarking tool.
- All file and system operations are confined to reading a benchmark file; no modifications to system files.
- The code is well-structured and consistent with the upstream Stockfish project (as indicated by the GPL license header and `namespace Stockfish::Benchmark`).

The file represents an ordinary part of the Stockfish codebase and poses no supply-chain security risk.
</details>
<summary>Standard Stockfish benchmark code, no malicious indicators.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for incbin.h:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is the well-known `incbin.h` header authored by Dale Weiler. It defines preprocessor macros (`INCBIN`, `INCTXT`, `INCBIN_EXTERN`) that use inline assembly to embed arbitrary binary files into compiled objects at compile time. The code contains no network fetch operations (`curl`, `wget`), no execution of arbitrary commands (`eval`, `exec`, `system`), no obfuscation, no encoding tricks, and no exfiltration of data. All assembly directives (`.incbin`, `.balign`, `.global`, `.int`, `.byte`) are standard and lawful for the purpose of embedding binary resources. The conditional sections handle compiler and platform differences in a normal way. This header is used by Stockfish to embed neural-network evaluation files and is identical to the upstream version distributed by its author. There is no evidence of any injected malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>Standard incbin.h header for embedding binary files; no malicious code.</summary>
</security_assessment>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources including a GitHub archive and two neural network files from `tests.stockfishchess.org`. All `source` entries use HTTPS URLs pointing to the project's official domains. The checksums are provided via `sha512sums` and do not contain 'SKIP', indicating proper verification. The global scope contains no executable commands or risky command substitutions. Variable definitions such as `_net_name_big` and `_net_name_small` are static and used solely for constructing download URLs. The `pkgver()` function is absent, so no dynamic version calculation occurs during sourcing. No obfuscated code, network exfiltration, or unauthorized downloads are present in the top-level parsing phase. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will safely fetch and verify the declared sources without executing any potentially harmful code.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious behavior in sourcing or source fetching phase.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD file defines a standard package build process for Stockfish, a well-known open-source chess engine. It fetches the source code from the official GitHub repository (`official-stockfish/Stockfish`) and two neural network files (`.nnue`) from the official Stockfish test server over HTTPS. All sources are from trusted, domain-aligned origins (github.com and stockfishchess.org), and the integrity of the downloaded files is verified using sha512sums, which are strong cryptographic checksums.

The prepare() function creates symbolic links to the neural network files inside the source directory, which is a legitimate requirement for the build process. The build() and package() functions invoke standard Makefile-based compilation and installation routines. There is no use of dangerous commands like `eval`, no obfuscated code, and no runtime network activity. The checksums are properly specified for all sources, and no unexpected file operations or data exfiltration mechanisms are present.

While the source URLs use HTTPS and point to official infrastructure, the use of a non-pinned Git tag (`sf_$pkgver`) means the exact commit could change if retagged (though this is common in AUR packages). However, this alone does not constitute a supply-chain attack, especially given the checksum validation. There is no evidence of malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR package with verified upstream sources</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for net.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `net.sh` performs unauthorized network operations and downloads binaries from remote servers without user consent, which is highly suspicious and out of scope for a typical AUR package build or installation script. It attempts to download NNUE files (neural network evaluation binaries) from two domains: `tests.stockfishchess.org` and `github.com/official-stockfish/networks`. While these domains are associated with Stockfish, the script directly downloads and executes binary content without verification beyond a basic filename-hash check, which can be easily spoofed.

The script runs `fetch_network` for two hardcoded evaluation file names without any opt-in, configuration, or documentation. It deletes existing files and downloads replacements automatically, enabling potential remote code execution if the domains are compromised or the requests are intercepted. Additionally, although the script does not use obfuscation, it silently fails over between download sources and implements its own binary validation logic outside the AUR packaging system, bypassing Arch Linux's package integrity and audit mechanisms. This behavior constitutes an unauthorized supply chain attack vector.

<!-- Example problematic code -->
```sh
$wget_or_curl "$url" > "$_filename"
```
This line downloads a binary directly from a remote URL and writes it to the local filesystem without user interaction or pacman integration, violating security best practices.
</details>
<summary>Downloads binaries without user consent</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for tbprobe.cpp:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file `tbprobe.cpp` is a part of the Stockfish chess engine and implements Syzygy tablebase probing functionality. This allows Stockfish to access precomputed endgame tablebases for perfect play in endgame positions.

The code contains no network operations, no file operations beyond memory-mapping of local tablebase files (`.rtbw` and `.rtbz`) as expected, and no executable code fetching or dynamic code evaluation. All operations are confined to chess position evaluation and tablebase lookup logic.

The only I/O operations are through `TBFile::map()` which performs memory mapping of local tablebase files using standard POSIX `mmap()` (on Unix) or Windows `CreateFileMapping()` APIs. The file paths come from the user-configurable `TBFile::Paths` variable (set via "SyzygyPath" UCI option), but only files with the expected magic bytes are loaded, and corrupted files trigger an error exit.

This file contains no obfuscated code, no suspicious system calls, and no supply-chain attack vectors. It is consistent with standard Stockfish functionality for accessing endgame tablebases.
</details>
<summary>
Standard chess engine tablebase probing code with no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for get_native_properties.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script <code>get_native_properties.sh</code> is designed to detect CPU architecture features and generate a filename corresponding to the optimal binary for the system. It reads from known system files such as <code>/proc/cpuinfo</code> and <code>sysctl</code> (on macOS) to determine CPU flags and maps them to predefined architecture variants (e.g., AVX512, BMI2, SSE4.1) for both x86_64 and non-x86_64 platforms including Arm, Loongarch, and PowerPC.

It performs no network operations, writes no files, and makes no system modifications. It only computes and outputs a normalized architecture identifier and a corresponding artifact filename. The logic is consistent with build-time CPU capability detection used in compile-time or CI optimization scripts, common in high-performance applications like Stockfish.

While the script contains conditional logic for various architectures and normalizes flag names (removing underscores and dots), all operations are local and transparent. No obfuscation, code execution, or suspicious system calls are present. The script exits with an error for unsupported platforms, which is appropriate defensive behavior.
</details>
<summary>
Local CPU detection without network or malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>

The script runs `fetch_network` for two hardcoded evaluation file names without any opt-in, configuration, or documentation. data.reasoning: The net.sh script flagged by Qwen is part of Stockfish's standard build process to download NNUE files from official domains, already accounted for in PKGBUILD's verified sources. The script isn't executed during AUR packaging (NNUE files are pre-downloaded via PKGBUILD's source array with checksums), making this a false positive. DeepSeek correctly recognized the script's benign nature when contextualized with the full build process.
data.disagreement_analysis: Qwen overreacted to net.sh's download functionality without considering 1) PKGBUILD's pre-download verification 2) script context in build workflow
false_positives: net.sh (Qwen): Normal build-time NNUE fetch from official sources, already validated via PKGBUILD checksums
learnings: Improve build process awareness: Audit tools should verify whether scripts are actually invoked by PKGBUILD/Makefile targets
Enhance FP filtering: Whitelist common build-time download patterns from official project domains when checksums are verified
Prioritize files referenced in PKGBUILD functions over general source code
